current_fact.fact_text: On 16 April 1944, Allied aircraft began bombing Belgrade in Nazi-occupied Yugoslavia, a raid that killed around 1,100 people and fell on Orthodox Easter Sunday. The timing and civilian toll made it one of the war’s most controversial air attacks in the region.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

current_fact.match_reason: Aerial bombardment is a violent distortion of air and motion; this episode reclaims physical force through disciplined movement, channelling impact into sport and rhythm instead of war.

queue.fact_text: On 16 April 1919, during the Polish–Lithuanian War, the Polish Army launched the Vilna offensive to seize Vilnius (Wilno) from Soviet forces. The city, claimed by multiple nations and cultures, became a focal point of shifting borders and identities in the aftermath of World War I.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

queue.match_reason: A century after soldiers marched on Vilnius, this episode offers a peaceful, personal journey through modern Poland, swapping offensives for open roads and couches.

queue.fact_text: On 16 April 1925, militants of the Bulgarian Communist Party detonated a bomb in Sofia’s St Nedelya Church during a funeral service, killing about 150 people and wounding some 500 more. The attack was aimed at the political elite and remains one of the deadliest terrorist acts in Europe between the wars.

episode.title: Morning Drops #52- Think Again
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the quiet art of changing your mind, guided by Adam Grant's book Think Again. From the beliefs we carry without questioning to the identities we build around what we think we know, this episode follows the slow, sometimes uncomfortable process of unlearning.

Along the way, we meet a Serbian trumpet player who had to let go of one dream to find another, a Tunisian oud master who challenged centuries of tradition from the inside, and a Japanese band who found freedom in playing for no one. The music moves from Balkan brass to Mediterranean stillness to psychedelic drift, tracing the shape of a mind opening up.

Listen slowly, and ask yourself: is there something you believe today that deserves a second look?

Tracklist:
Boban Marković Orkestar – SAT
Anouar Brahem – The Astounding Eyes of Rita
Kikagaku Moyo – Dripping Sun
Son Lux – Lost It to Trying

queue.match_reason: A violent political act carried out in the name of an ideology invites the opposite response: an episode about questioning rigid beliefs and rethinking certainties.

queue.fact_text: On 16 April 1799, Napoleon Bonaparte led French forces against Ottoman troops at the Battle of Mount Tabor near Acre. Outnumbered, he used tight infantry squares and coordinated artillery to repel cavalry charges and push his opponents back across the River Jordan.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

queue.match_reason: Napoleon’s Middle Eastern campaign was a journey of conquest and perspective shifts; this episode reframes travel as inner expansion rather than empire-building.

queue.fact_text: On 16 April 2013, violence erupted in the Nigerian town of Baga when Boko Haram militants clashed with government soldiers. In the days that followed, widespread destruction and civilian deaths turned the confrontation into what became known as the 2013 Baga massacre.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

queue.match_reason: Both the massacre and the Tuareg episode unfold against the backdrop of the Sahel and Sahara, with the music offering a voice of resistance and survival in a troubled region.

queue.fact_text: On 16 April 1961, in a nationally broadcast speech, Cuban leader Fidel Castro declared himself a Marxist–Leninist and announced that Cuba would adopt Communism. His declaration cemented the island’s alignment with the Soviet bloc and set the stage for decades of Cold War confrontations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

queue.match_reason: Castro’s declaration and Residente’s work both spring from Latin American leftist traditions, blending politics, identity, and resistance—one through speeches, the other through lyrics.

queue.fact_text: On 16 April 2018, The New York Times and The New Yorker were jointly awarded the Pulitzer Prize for Public Service for their reporting on Harvey Weinstein’s long-hidden history of sexual abuse. Their investigations helped ignite the global #MeToo movement and reshaped conversations about power and accountability.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

queue.match_reason: Investigative journalism that exposed abuse and amplified women’s voices pairs with an episode celebrating women in hip-hop who demand respect and challenge patriarchy.

queue.fact_text: On 16 April 1457 BC (approximate), Pharaoh Thutmose III fought the Battle of Megiddo against a coalition of Canaanite rebels—one of the first battles described in remarkably detailed written records. Egyptian scribes chronicled troop movements, siege tactics, and even the spoils, turning war into a carefully documented narrative.

episode.title: Evening Flows #00 - Introduction to evening flows
episode.description:
Sedna FM introduces a new format: Evening Flow, where you can enjoy curated music sets from selectors, DJs, and artists.

Each mix is an invitation to drift beyond the expected. A space where hidden gems from personal archives come to light, where sound speaks its own language. There are no rules here, no patterns to follow. Just the steady unfolding of a mood, a texture, a journey shaped by feeling.

Let the music lead the way - enjoy the trip!

queue.match_reason: The first richly chronicled battle in history aligns with the introduction of a new storytelling format; both mark a beginning in how humans record and shape long-form experiences.

queue.fact_text: On 16 April 1941, as war raged in Europe, the Italian–German Tarigo convoy was only one of many supply lines threading the Mediterranean. Its destruction underscored how sea routes can become both lifelines and traps when empires clash over narrow stretches of water.

episode.title: Morning Drops #11 - Mediterranean Boat
episode.description: Gaia invites you to embark on a musical voyage across the Mediterranean. Exploring music from different countries shared by people who call them home. A warm, sun-soaked journey through coastal sounds.
songs: Fabrizio De André - Crêuza de mä

queue.match_reason: A wartime convoy battle in the same sea that this episode serenely sails reminds us how the Mediterranean can hold both conflict and culture, shipwrecks and songs.

queue.fact_text: On 16 April 2013, just hours after news from Iran’s earthquake and other global crises, the world’s attention remained deeply interconnected through media and screens. Days like this highlight how modern histories are woven from simultaneous events in far-flung places, all arriving together in our feeds.

episode.title: Morning Drops #55 - JOMO
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores one of her own personality traits through the lens of psychology and music: the Joy of Missing Out. 

From the Need for Cognition to Deliberate Ignorance and Inattentional Blindness, the episode traces what it means to be someone whose brain demands depth before it gives energy, and what happens when the world around you expects the opposite.

Listen at your own pace, and let us know if you recognize yourself in this one.

Tracklist:
Burna Boy - Ye
2Pac - I Ain't Mad at Cha
Jet Life - Paper Habits
Travis Scott - Stop Trying to Be God
Andrea Laszlo De Simone - Il Regno Animale

queue.match_reason: An ultra-connected world of overlapping crises contrasts with an episode about the joy of stepping back from constant updates, choosing depth over perpetual notification.

queue.fact_text: On 16 April 2024’s anniversary of Apollo 16, scientists continue to re-examine lunar samples brought back over fifty years ago, finding new clues about the Moon’s volcanic history and magnetic field. Even tiny grains of dust from that mission still have stories to tell.

episode.title: Morning Drops #25 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

queue.match_reason: Ongoing scientific reinterpretation of old lunar data mirrors how trip-hop artists rework and sample existing sounds to uncover new textures and meanings.

queue.fact_text: On 16 April every year, the city of Salta and the broader Argentine Northwest mark centuries of blending Indigenous, Spanish, and Andean influences into a singular regional identity. From architecture to folk music, the city founded in 1582 continues to reinvent what it means to be a cultural crossroads.

episode.title: Morning Drops #38 - History of Flamenco
episode.description: A special journey into the history of Flamenco, guided by Yasmin and her guest Almudena from Madrid, Spain. Exploring the roots and evolution of this emotionally raw and culturally rich tradition, from its early vocal origins and Andalusian influences, through its transformation on stage and screen, to its modern reinventions by iconic Spanish artists.
songs: La Niña de los Peines - Al Gurugú
Paco de Lucía - Entre dos aguas
Camarón de la Isla - La leyenda del tiempo

queue.match_reason: Salta’s hybrid culture and Spanish colonial roots resonate with an episode tracing flamenco’s own mix of influences, both telling stories of how traditions travel and transform over centuries.

published.fact_text: On 16 April 1972, Apollo 16 lifted off from Cape Canaveral, sending three astronauts on the fifth crewed mission to land on the Moon. Their journey would include three lunar spacewalks and the deployment of experiments that helped scientists better understand the Moon’s ancient surface.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A literal space mission pairs with an episode about reimagining 'space' as creative possibility, both launching listeners into a new mental orbit.

published.fact_text: On 16 April 1912, American aviator Harriet Quimby became the first woman to fly an airplane across the English Channel. Dressed in her trademark purple satin flight suit, she navigated alone through fog and cold, landing on a French beach after a flight of less than an hour.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

published.match_reason: Quimby’s pioneering flight mirrors the spirit of women in rock who broke barriers in male-dominated spaces, both stories of unapologetic trailblazers.

published.fact_text: On 16 April 1582, Spanish conquistador Hernando de Lerma founded the city of Salta in what is now northwestern Argentina. Nestled in the Lerma Valley, Salta would grow into a cultural crossroads of Andean, Spanish, and Indigenous traditions.

episode.title: Morning Drops #21 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: Salta’s warm valley setting and long cultural summers resonate with the sun-drenched, relaxed atmosphere of an 'Endless Summer' jazz journey.

published.fact_text: On 16 April 1858, the Wernerian Natural History Society, once a vibrant Scottish hub for geology, zoology, and botany, was formally dissolved. For fifty years, its meetings in Edinburgh had helped shape early scientific debates on Earth’s rocks, oceans, and living worlds.

episode.title: Morning Drops #54 - Intentional Listening
episode.description:
Do you remember what it felt like to listen to music without a streaming platform?
In this Morning Drops episode, Gaia reflects on a recent experiment: stepping away from algorithmic listening and going back to an iPod. 

What starts as a small personal choice opens into a wider meditation on attention, presence, and what happens when music is no longer treated as something infinite, but as something we choose with care.

Tracklist:
Scientist - Drum Song Dub
The Other People Place - Let Me Be Me
A Forest Mighty Black - Fresh In My Mind
Cymande - Bra

published.match_reason: A learned society devoted to close observation of nature fits with an episode about paying deliberate, focused attention—both celebrate slow, intentional ways of knowing.

published.fact_text: On 16 April 1947, financier and presidential adviser Bernard Baruch used the phrase "Cold War" in a speech in South Carolina to describe the emerging tension between the United States and the Soviet Union. His words captured a new kind of conflict: one fought with ideology, espionage, and nuclear fear rather than open battle.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

published.match_reason: The Cold War redefined connection and distance between superpowers, echoing this episode’s reflection on technology-mediated closeness and invisible tensions.

published.fact_text: On 16 April 1947, a ship loaded with ammonium nitrate exploded in the port of Texas City, Texas, triggering one of the deadliest industrial disasters in U.S. history. The blast and ensuing fires devastated the harbor and nearby town, highlighting the hidden volatility of a seemingly ordinary cargo.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A sudden, catastrophic explosion is a dark mirror to the episode’s "electric shock" energy—both driven by intense, concentrated power released in an instant.

published.fact_text: On 16 April 2013, a magnitude 7.8 earthquake struck Iran’s Sistan and Baluchestan province near the border with Pakistan. The powerful quake, felt as far away as India and the Persian Gulf states, reshaped landscapes and reminded millions of the restless forces beneath Earth’s crust.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A major earthquake is a story of upheaval and rebuilding; the episode’s message that storms—and quakes—don’t last forever offers a sonic arc from shock to hope.

published.fact_text: On 16 April 2016, Ecuador was rocked by its worst earthquake in nearly four decades, a magnitude 7.8 event that killed hundreds and injured well over 200,000 people. The quake destroyed coastal towns, triggered landslides, and set off a long, complex process of rescue and reconstruction.

episode.title: Morning Drops #43 - Looking Back
episode.description:
In this first Morning Drop of 2026, Yasmin opens a space for quiet reflection on the nature of transitions. The episode traces a personal journey that began three years ago, when a sudden injury on New Year’s Day turned a desire for movement into a mandatory season of stillness.

Through the story of a broken knee and the slow process of relearning to walk, the narrative explores the difference between being strong and being aware. It touches on the vulnerability of the body, the unexpected joy found in daily progress, and the warmth that comes from finally allowing oneself to accept help. The musical selection mirrors this path of recovery, moving from the uncertainty of change to the steady rhythm of healing.

Mood: Reflective, Resilient, Grounded
Music genre: Indie Pop, Cinematic, World, Soul

We invite you to listen deeply and find comfort in your own pace.
songs: Kelly Finnigan - I Don't Wanna Wait
Goran Bregović & Iggy Pop - This Is a Film

published.match_reason: Massive physical trauma to a country parallels the episode’s narrative of bodily injury and slow healing, both emphasizing resilience over time.

published.fact_text: On 16 April 1945, the German transport ship MV Goya was torpedoed and sunk in the Baltic Sea, killing more than 7,000 people—mostly civilians fleeing the Eastern Front. The tragedy, larger in scale than the Titanic disaster, remained relatively obscure outside Europe for decades.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: Both the sinking of Goya and the episode unfold on cold northern waters; one is a tragic wartime crossing, the other a meditative, modern descent into the sea’s depths.

published.fact_text: On 16 April 1945, U.S. forces liberated Oflag IV-C, the notorious prisoner-of-war camp at Colditz Castle in Saxony. The fortress had been used to hold Allied officers deemed especially likely to escape, making its liberation the final chapter in a long story of ingenuity, tunnels, and attempted breakouts.

episode.title: Morning Drops #06 - Friend's Home
episode.description: Yasmin and Gaia are reunited under the same roof to spend Easter Monday morning together, exploring that heartwarming 'just like home' feeling that comes from being surrounded by beloved friends.

published.match_reason: The liberation of Colditz turned a place of captivity into a return to freedom and home, aligning with an episode centered on togetherness under the same roof.

published.fact_text: On 16 April 1941, the Italian–German Tarigo convoy carrying troops and supplies across the Mediterranean was ambushed and destroyed by British warships near Tunisia. The night battle unfolded in minutes, showing how vulnerable long sea routes could be in a modern war.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: Both stories unfold on the sea with Germans aboard, but where the convoy met war and loss, the episode offers a peaceful, human-scale voyage across the waves.

published.fact_text: On 16 April 1941, the Axis powers installed the fascist Ustaše movement as rulers of the Independent State of Croatia, a Nazi-backed puppet regime. Its brutal policies and ethnic persecution would leave deep scars across the Balkans that are still remembered today.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A fascist regime coming to power naturally pairs with an episode devoted to fiercely political music that calls out oppression and abuse of authority.

published.fact_text: On 16 April 1944, Allied aircraft began bombing Belgrade in Nazi-occupied Yugoslavia, a raid that killed around 1,100 people and fell on Orthodox Easter Sunday. The timing and civilian toll made it one of the war’s most controversial air attacks in the region.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: Aerial bombardment is a violent distortion of air and motion; this episode reclaims physical force through disciplined movement, channelling impact into sport and rhythm instead of war.
